title: Chapter 4: The Marcus Incident
content: <!-- Speed: Explosion | D4+D7 -->
"You're late."

Marcus Holt didn't look up when she walked in. He was leaning back in his chair, Italian leather creaking, scrolling through something on his tablet with one finger. His office was all glass and sharp angles — a corner unit on the fourteenth floor with a view of the bridge that probably cost more per square foot than Wren's entire apartment.
She wasn't late. She was four minutes early. She'd checked her phone twice on the elevator.
"I said you're late, Wren. Did you hear me?"
"I heard you."
"Great. Then sit."

She sat. The chair across from Marcus's desk was low and firm and positioned so that she had to look up at him. She knew this was intentional. Everything in this room was intentional — the framed awards on the wall, the signed Basquiat print that was probably fake, the way his desk was positioned to catch the light from the floor-to-ceiling windows so that his face was always half in shadow.

Marcus set the tablet down. He was a big man, not tall but wide, with the kind of presence that filled a room by leaning into it. His hair was silver-blond, cut short. His jaw was square. He wore his watch on the inside of his wrist — a power move she'd read about once in a body-language book and never forgotten.
"Let's talk about the Harper campaign."
"Right, I —"

"Because frankly, Wren, what you sent over isn't there yet. The linework is muddy. The palette is — I don't know what the palette is. Sad? It's sad. The whole thing reads sad, and Harper is not a sad brand. Harper is aspirational. Harper is the woman who has her life together. Your illustrations look like they were drawn by someone who doesn't have her life together."
Wren's fingers pressed into her thigh under the table. She felt the familiar heat climbing her neck, the tightening in her throat that made speech feel like swallowing glass.
"I can revise —"

"Can you?" Marcus leaned forward. "Because I've seen three rounds now, and I'm starting to wonder if you're right for this. Honestly. I'm starting to wonder if you're right for any of it. The agency took a chance on you — I took a chance on you — and what I'm getting back is... what? Mood boards? Sketches?"
"They're finished —"
"They're not finished. Finished is when I don't have to look at them and think, 'God, do I need to hold her hand through this?'"
The room was very quiet. Outside, the city hummed fourteen floors below. A siren, maybe. Traffic. The low white noise of money being made.

Wren stared at the desk. Marcus's coffee sat in a ceramic mug — not a paper cup, a real mug, matte black with GOLDMAN SACHES in raised letters on the side. A souvenir from a previous life, a career before he pivoted to creative direction and started making people like Wren feel like they weremade of paper.
"Your portfolio was better than this," Marcus said. "Whatever you sent me when we first signed you — that had energy. This? This is someone going through the motions."
Seven minutes. He'd been talking for seven minutes and she hadn't gotten a word in.
"Let me try again," she said.
"You're going to try again regardless. What I'm asking is whether I should expect anything different."
She didn't answer. The silence swelled. Marcus exhaled through his nose — a deliberate sound, a sound he made on purpose to fill the gap with his disappointment.
"Fine. Next round by Friday. And Wren —"
She looked up.
"Bring your A-game. Or don't bring anything."

He turned back to his tablet. Dismissed. A piece of furniture that had briefly made a noise. Wren's hands were shaking. She pressed them flat against her thighs and felt the tremor transfer to the fabric of her jeans.
She stood. The chair scraped the floor. Marcus didn't react.
That was when she noticed.

He really wasn't looking at her. Not in the dismissive way — not in the I'm-done-with-you way she'd experienced a hundred times. He was reading his tablet, his finger scrolling, his face arranged in the particular blankness of someone who believed they were alone.
She was still standing directly in front of his desk.
He should have seen her move. Standing up was movement. The chair scraping was sound. But Marcus scrolled and sipped his coffee and seemed to exist in a bubble that she was not inside.
Wren took a step to the right. Then another. She moved laterally along the front of his desk, within arm's reach of his keyboard, his coffee, the matte-black mug with its stupid raised letters.
Marcus sipped his coffee. He looked left — through the window. He looked right — at his phone. He did not look at Wren.
She reached out and picked up his coffee cup.
The mug was warm. Weight in her hand. Steam curling from the surface. She held it six inches above the desk, directly in Marcus's line of sight.
He kept scrolling.
She moved the cup to the left side of the desk. Set it down on a stack of Post-its. The paper crinkled under the ceramic base.
Marcus reached for his coffee. His hand went to the right side of the desk — where the cup had been — and touched empty space. His fingers splayed on the blotter. He frowned.
He looked left. Saw the cup on the Post-it stack. His frown deepened.
"Jesus," he muttered. He pulled the cup back to its original position and took a sip.

Wren's heart was hammering. Not anxiety — something else. Something electric and sharp. She moved around the desk. Stood behind him. He didn't turn. She leaned close enough to smell his cologne — oud and bergamot, expensive, the kind that comes in a bottle the size of a fist.
"I'm here," she said. Not loud. Conversational volume.
Marcus didn't flinch. Didn't blink. Didn't turn.
She walked back around the desk. Stood in front of him. Picked up a pen from his organizer — a Montblanc, the oversized kind, worth three hundred dollars minimum. She held it in both hands.
And she snapped it in half.
The crack was loud. Sharp. In the quiet office, it was a gunshot. Ink splattered across the desk — black, viscous, blooming on the white blotter like a Rorschach test.
Marcus jumped. Literally jumped — shoulders up, chair back, hands off the tablet. He stared at the broken pen. At the ink. At the stain spreading across his desk.
"What the —"

He looked around the room. His gaze swept over Wren without stopping. Swept the windows, the door, the corners. He was looking for the source of the sound. Looking for a person, an intruder, an explanation.
His gaze landed on Wren and kept moving.
"Who's there?" His voice was harder now, a thin shell of authority over something less stable. He stood. The chair rolled back and hit the window wall. "I know someone's here. Show yourself."
Wren stood three feet from him. She was breathing hard. Her hands were streaked with black ink. The two halves of the pen were still in her fists.
She raised her right hand. Slowly. Deliberately. She placed the broken pen half on the desk.

Marcus watched the pen half appear — materialize, from his perspective — on the surface of his desk. His face did something remarkable. The authority cracked. Underneath was something raw and unfinished: confusion, then a flutter of genuine fear.
"Okay." He said it to the empty room. "Okay, that's — that's not possible."

Wren picked up his mug. The matte-black GOLDMAN SACHES mug. She carried it to the far side of the office. She placed it on the bookshelf, between a set of design annuals and a framed photo of Marcus shaking hands with a mayor.

When Marcus turned and saw his coffee mug on the bookshelf, he made a sound — not a word, not a scream, just a vocalized short-circuit. A hum of disbelief. He crossed the room in three strides and grabbed the mug, stared into it — coffee still warm, still his, still real — and set it down hard on the nearest surface.
"The fuck," he said. "The actual fuck."
He went to his desk phone. Punched a button. "Mira. Mira, get in here."

The door opened. Marcus's assistant stood in the doorway — early thirties, dark hair pulled back, wearing the specific expression of someone who has been summoned in a tone that meant trouble. She looked at Marcus. She looked at the ink-splattered desk, the broken pen, the stains.
"Did you see anyone come in?" Marcus's voice was tight. Controlled. Barely.
Mira blinked. "Just — your ten o'clock. Wren? She left a few minutes ago, I thought."
"I'm telling you, she didn't leave. Something is — someone was just in here. Things are moving. My coffee was across the room. My pen — look at my pen."
Mira looked at the pen. At the ink. At Marcus's face. She was performing a calculation — determining whether her boss was having a breakdown or a prank.
"Marcus, I didn't see anyone else go in or come out. Maybe you knocked the coffee over yourself and —"
"I didn't knock anything over. The pen snapped. I heard it snap. I heard it, Mira."

Wren stood between them. Mira's gaze passed over her twice — once scanning the room, once confirming the room was empty. It was like watching a searchlight sweep over a rock in a field. The light hits, the light moves. The rock stays.

She walked to the desk. Slowly, now. Testing. She picked up a stack of Marcus's design proofs and shuffled them — reversed the order, put the bottom one on top. She moved his keyboard an inch to the left. She straightened the framed photo of his daughter that sat at the corner of his monitor.

Marcus watched the objects rearrange themselves. He didn't speak. His mouth was open and his hands were flat on the desk and he was watching reality edit itself in real time and he had no framework for it, no category, no place in his worldview for things that move on their own.

Mira was still in the doorway. She had seen the proofs shift — she must have, the movement was right there — but her brain was doing what brains do: fitting the anomaly into an acceptable narrative. A draft. A tremor. Something.
"Marcus," she said carefully. "Do you want me to call someone?"
"No. Yes. I don't — no. Just — close the door on your way out."

Mira closed the door. Her face, in the half-second before the door shut, held a specific expression: the confused concern of a person who has witnessed something wrong and chosen, for now, to explain it away.
Wren was alone with Marcus.

He sat down. His hands were shaking. He was trying to hide it — pressing his palms flat on the desk, clenching, releasing — but they shook. The man who had spent ten minutes making Wren feel like furniture was sitting at his own desk, afraid of his own office.
She picked up the Montblanc pen. Both halves. She tucked them into her hoodie pocket.

Then she walked to the security camera in the corner — the small black dome mounted where wall met ceiling. She looked up at it. The red light was on. Recording. She held up one ink-stained hand and flipped off the lens.

Later — an hour later, after she'd walked out through the lobby and past the security desk and into the bright afternoon — she would think about the security footage. She would pull up the building's app on her phone, the one that let tenants review lobby camera feeds, and she would find the timestamp for Marcus's office and she would watch the footage from the fourteenth floor and see:

Marcus, alone at his desk. Talking to an empty chair. Making expressions that ranged from irritation to fear. His coffee cup teleporting from desk to bookshelf. His pen exploding. His proofs shuffling like a card trick. Objects moving as if manipulated by invisible hands.

And in the corner of the frame — where Wren should have been — nothing. Not a blur, not a shadow, not a smudge. Just pixel-smear. A faint digital noise, like static, like the camera couldn't resolve whatever was standing in that space. As if the algorithm that carved human shapes out of background data had looked at Wren Walker and returned null.
She closed the app. She was sitting on a bench outside the building, half a block from the lobby doors. Her hands were still ink-stained. The broken pen halves were in her pocket.

Inside the building, Marcus Holt was probably still sitting at his desk, trying to convince himself that he was sane. Trying to build a rational explanation for things that moved when he wasn't looking, for sounds without sources, for a room that didn't follow the rules.

Wren took a breath. Let it out. Her heart was still fast, but it wasn't the old rhythm — not the anxious stutter she'd carried for twenty-four years. This was different. This was the pulse of someone who had just discovered that the walls of the cage were made of paper and she'd been a bird the whole time.
She'd been erased. She was furious. She was free.

She got up and walked away from the building, and the automatic doors at the lobby exit opened for her — perfectly, instantly, without that half-second hesitation — and the sunlight fell on her skin and her shadow stretched out long and thin on the sidewalk behind her, and for one block, for one bright unexamined block, she let herself believe that she was the one who had chosen this.
